chunks.text: Cal’s Mu Chapter receives IEEE-HKN Outstanding Chapter Award. The Mu Chapter of UC Berkeley has received the 2014-15 IEEE-HKN Outstanding Chapter Award. This award is presented to IEEE-HKN chapters in recognition of excellence in their chapter administration and programs. Recipients are selected on the basis of improving professional development; raising instructional and institutional standards; encouraging scholarship and creativity.
chunks.question: What award did UC Berkeley's Mu Chapter receive?

chunks.text: Paper by Akkaya, Fremont, Valle, Donze, Lee and Seshia wins IEEE IoTDI Best Paper Award. The paper “Control Improvisation with Probabilistic Temporal Specifications,” co-authored by Ilge Akkaya, Daniel Fremont, Rafael Valle (graduate students), Alexandre Donze (postdoctoral researcher) and Professors Edward A. Lee and Sanjit A. Seshia, based on research conducted in the TerraSwarm Research Center, received the Best Paper Award at the IEEE IoTDI conference.
chunks.question: Which paper won the IEEE IoTDI Best Paper Award?
chunks.answer: Control Improvisation with Probabilistic Temporal Specifications

chunks.text: 11 EECS Grad Students Awarded NSF GRFP Fellowships. 11 EECS graduate students have been awarded National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowships (NSF GRFP). In EE they are Sidney Douglas Buchbinder, Regina Eckert, Laura Hallock, Sang Min Han, Michael Kellman, Efthymios Papageorgiou and Margaret Payne. In CS they are Abhishek Gupta, Grant Ho, Ethan Jackson and Gregory Kahn.
chunks.question: How many EECS graduate students received NSF GRFP fellowships?

chunks.text: Cameron Rose’s robotic birds mimic flight. EECS graduate student Cameron Rose is featured in a National Geographic article titled “Dreams of the World: Flight Simulation of Robotic Birds with Cameron Rose, UC Berkeley”. Cameron, who is in Prof. Ron Fearing’s Biomimetic Millisystems Laboratory, has helped design and build the mechanical bird called the H2 Bird. His research focuses on robotic flight simulation.
chunks.question: What is the name of Cameron Rose's robotic bird?

chunks.text: Kay Ousterhout chosen for Google Ph.D. Fellowship. Kay Ousterhout recently won the Google Ph.D. Fellowship. This is one of the highest honors a CS grad student can win. It is extremely selective, with only a small number universities invited to submit two nominees each. Kay is a 5th year student of Prof. Sylvia Ratnasamy.
chunks.question: Which fellowship did Kay Ousterhout receive?

chunks.text: Papers from EECS research groups featured in Proceedings of Electronic Design Automation journal. A special issue of the Proceedings of the IEEE, the most highly cited general-interest journal in electrical engineering and computer science, on the evolution of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) and its future developments, features papers from a number of research groups in EECS. The issue includes Prof. Robert Brayton's work.
chunks.question: Which journal featured EECS research on Electronic Design Automation?

chunks.text: Numbers of Underrepresented Groups Grow in EECS. In 2011 EECS partnered with the campus Division of Equity & Inclusion on a new strategic planning initiative to improve the numbers and create a more welcoming environment for women and underrepresented groups in STEM fields. Today, thanks to the founding work of this initiative, there has been growth in underrepresented groups within EECS.
chunks.question: What initiative improved diversity in EECS?

chunks.text: Pejman Mar Ventures creates new competition for UC Berkeley entrepreneurs. Venture capital firm Pejman Mar Ventures has created a new competition for UC Berkeley entrepreneurs who demonstrate passion and dedication to making their vision a reality. They are offering $250,000 to back up a startup founded by a Berkeley student, faculty member or alum in exchange for 10 percent equity.
chunks.question: What does Pejman Mar Ventures offer in their competition?
